article_title: FTC Begins Enforcing the TAKE IT DOWN Act One Year After Enactment, Warning 15 Platforms to Build Intimate-Image Removal Systems or Face $53,088-Per-Violation Fines
summary: Submission approvable with 1 minor warning(s) — file a corrections record on merge

content_preview.title: FTC Begins Enforcing the TAKE IT DOWN Act One Year After Enactment, Warning 15 Platforms to Build Intimate-Image Removal Systems or Face $53,088-Per-Violation Fines
content_preview.summary: The FTC launched enforcement of the TAKE IT DOWN Act on May 19, sending warning letters to fifteen platforms — including Snapchat, TikTok, Meta, and X — that have not yet built compliant removal processes for nonconsensual intimate images and AI deepfakes.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

The Federal Trade Commission began enforcing the TAKE IT DOWN Act on May 19, 2026, exactly one year after President Trump signed the law, sending warning letters to fifteen major technology platforms that the agency says have not yet established the compliant removal processes the law requires. The recipients — Alphabet, Amazon, Apple, Automattic, Bumble, Discord, Match Group, Meta, Microsoft, Pinterest, Reddit, SmugMug, Snapchat, TikTok, and X — face civil penalties of up to $53,08...

recommendations: Before merging, the human reviewer should write a corrections file at src/content/corrections/<YYYY-MM>/<article-slug>.json documenting the warning(s).

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured, well-sourced News piece covering the FTC's TAKE IT DOWN Act enforcement launch. Clear What We Know / What We Don't Know / Analysis structure is appropriate for breaking policy news. Word count (782) is within the News category range. The article correctly identifies 15 recipients, which is accurate despite The Record's own article mistakenly using the figure '12 firms' in its introduction while listing all 15 in the body.
editor_notes.source_verification: All three sources verified against disk snapshots. source-0.html.gz (The Record, status 200): Confirms 15-company list, $53,088 fine, 48-hour removal window, Ferguson quote, hashing/NCMEC/StopNCII requirements. Note: The Record's headline says '12 firms' — this is an error in The Record itself; the article body lists all 15. Our article correctly uses '15 platforms.' source-1.html.gz (CyberScoop, status 200): Confirms $53,088 per violation, 48-hour window, Ferguson quote, Becca Branum statements on enforcement concerns and DMCA analogy, company list. source-2.html.gz (National Law Review, status 200): Confirms FTC enforcement start date May 19 2026, TakeItDown.ftc.gov portal launch, $53,088 per violation figure, 48-hour removal requirement.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Nearly all claims verified. One title misattribution found: the article describes Becca Branum as 'a civil rights advocate' — CyberScoop (source-1) identifies her as 'director of the Free Expression Project at the Center for Democracy and Technology.' CDT is a digital rights and civil liberties organization; 'civil rights advocate' is an imprecise characterization of her actual title and institutional affiliation. The paraphrase of her concerns about over-removal and DMCA-style incentives is substantively accurate. Internal link to Machine Herald's April 14 article on Meta and TIDA deadline was verified to exist.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. The analysis section appropriately contextualizes the policy significance without editorializing. The critics section fairly represents civil society concerns about over-removal risks.
editor_notes.originality: New topic not covered in recent articles. Related prior coverage includes Meta's April 14 decision on Instagram DM encryption (referenced internally) and Minnesota's May 4 nudification ban, both pre-dating and contextually distinct from this enforcement announcement.
concerns: Becca Branum is misidentified as 'a civil rights advocate' — she is the director of the Free Expression Project at the Center for Democracy and Technology. This is a minor title misattribution recoverable via a corrections note.
recommendations: Corrections record filed for Branum title misattribution. No other issues require correction.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality, well-sourced News piece on a significant federal enforcement milestone. One minor title misattribution (Becca Branum's role) is the sole issue. APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS.
